1.pericope_label: High Priests and the Son of God
1.synthesis: Every high priest, chosen from among men, is appointed to act on behalf of men in relation to God — to offer gifts and sacrifices for sins, able to deal gently with the ignorant and wayward, since he himself is beset with weakness, and because of this must offer sacrifice for himself as well as for the people (vv.1–3). Moreover, no one takes this honor upon himself but is called by God, as Aaron was (v.4). So also Christ did not exalt himself to be made a high priest, but was appointed by the one who said to him: "You are my Son, today I have begotten you" (Psalm 2:7) and "You are a priest forever, after the order of Melchizedek" (Psalm 110:4) (vv.5–6). In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence — and although he was a Son, he learned obedience through what he suffered (vv.7–8). Being made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation to all who obey him, being designated by God a high priest after the order of Melchizedek (vv.9–10).
1.historical_context: MKT notes that Gethsemane is not explicitly mentioned but that the description of "loud cries and tears" to "him who was able to save him from death" is best understood as referring to Jesus's prayer in the garden (Mark 14:32–42; Matthew 26:36–46). The phrase "learned obedience from what he suffered" draws on the Greek pedagogical maxim pathei mathos ("learning through suffering"), attributed to Aeschylus (Agamemnon 177) and widely developed in Greek philosophical schools. The combination of Psalm 2:7 and Psalm 110:4 in vv.5–6 joins the royal and priestly messianic categories that the OT largely kept separate — Qumran expected two messiahs (one priestly, one Davidic); Hebrews presents one who fulfills both.
1.literary_structure: The section establishes the criteria for legitimate high-priestly appointment (vv.1–4) and then applies each criterion to Christ (vv.5–10). The criteria are: chosen from among men (v.1) → applied in Christ's incarnation (implied throughout); appointed by God, not self-appointed (v.4) → applied in the two Psalm citations (vv.5–6); able to sympathize because personally acquainted with weakness (vv.2–3) → applied in the Gethsemane-reference of vv.7–8. But Christ surpasses each criterion in the direction of superiority: he was appointed by a divine oath (not merely divine command), he did not offer for his own sins (unlike the Aaronic high priest who offered first for himself, v.3), and he was perfected through complete obedience to death (not through repeated rituals).
1.theological_application: Chrysostom on ch.5: "The blessed Paul wishes to show in the next place that this covenant is far better than the old... since there was nothing bodily or that made a show... but all things higher and more perfect." The argument is not that Aaron's priesthood was bad but that it was earthly and temporary — designed to teach by contrast what the heavenly and permanent priesthood of the Son would accomplish. Gill on "he was heard because of his reverence" (v.7): the Greek eulabeia is translated both "godly fear" and "reverence" — Christ's prayer in the garden was answered not by the removal of the cup but by his being sustained through it, which is the kind of hearing that matters for the priestly work he was accomplishing. "Being made perfect" (v.9) is the completion of his high-priestly qualifications through suffering — not moral perfection (he was sinless from the beginning) but vocational completion.
1.christology: The phrase "he learned obedience through what he suffered" (v.8) is one of the most theologically dense statements in the NT. It says simultaneously that he was a Son (never not the Son, never a merely human learner) and that he genuinely learned (the incarnate experience of obedience-under-pressure was real, not performance). Gill: "Though he were a Son — the Son of God... yet he learned obedience; not that he was disobedient before, but that he made it apparent that he was obedient." The learning was the experiential confirmation of what he already was — his life translated divine obedience into human form, step by step, suffering by suffering, until death. This is why he is the "source of eternal salvation" (v.9): not because he earned it by merit but because he is the one through whom obedience to God unto death was fully enacted in human nature.

11.synthesis: The author breaks off the Melchizedek discussion, saying: "About this we have much to say, and it is hard to explain, since you have become dull of hearing" (v.11). By this time the readers ought to be teachers, but instead they need someone to teach them again the basic principles of the oracles of God — they need milk, not solid food (v.12). Everyone on milk is unskilled in the word of righteousness, since they are infants — but solid food is for the mature, who by constant practice have trained their powers of discernment to distinguish good from evil (vv.13–14).
11.historical_context: MKT notes that the milk-vs.-solid-food metaphor for levels of teaching appears in Philo (On the Initial Studies 19; On Agriculture 9) in the context of philosophical pedagogy: beginners receive milk (preliminary instruction); the advanced receive solid food (metaphysical and theological knowledge). The same metaphor appears in 1 Corinthians 3:2 (Paul to the Corinthians) and 1 Peter 2:2 (positive use). The expectation of progressive theological formation was built into the structure of Jewish religious education — the bet midrash (house of study) alongside the synagogue provided ongoing study. Mishnaic tractate Avot describes successive stages of Jewish learning from age 5 (Bible) through 10 (Mishnah) through adult levels of deep interpretation.
11.literary_structure: The warning interrupts the Melchizedek discussion (which will resume in ch.7) and functions as a second warning passage (after 2:1–4 and 3:7–4:13), but one focused on epistemic failure rather than moral failure. The critique is not that the readers have sinned against the moral law but that they have failed to grow in knowledge — their spiritual immaturity makes them incapable of receiving what the author has to teach. The interruption is itself a rhetorical move: by pausing to name the problem, the author both creates urgency ("you need to grow up to receive what follows") and shames the mature readers into attention.
11.theological_application: Gill on v.12: "these Hebrews had had great advantages; they were not only descended from Abraham, and had the law of Moses, and the writings of the Old Testament, but some of them had enjoyed the ministry of Christ, and however of his apostles; and it was now about thirty years from the day of Pentecost." The failure is not of raw capacity but of development: they had time to grow and had not. Gill on v.14 on "trained powers of discernment": this is not innate moral intuition but cultivated skill — the Greek hexis (habit, settled disposition) describes what comes from repeated practice. The implication: the dangerous spiritual situation the Hebrews are in (tempted to apostasy) is partly a result of their failure to mature — mature believers who had developed discernment would be less susceptible to the attractions of returning to Judaism.
11.christology: The "word of righteousness" in v.13 that immature believers are unskilled in is, in Hebrews' context, the word of the Son's priestly righteousness — the message that his sacrifice achieved what the law's sacrifices could not. Immaturity in this word is specifically inability to understand the christological argument that chapters 7–10 will develop: that Jesus is the high priest after the order of Melchizedek, that his covenant is better, that his sacrifice is once for all. The Melchizedek discussion the author is about to attempt is "hard to explain" not because it is intellectually difficult in the abstract but because it requires a level of doctrinal formation about Christ that the readers have not developed. To receive the hard teaching about Christ's priestly supremacy, one must have grown in basic understanding of who he is.
